title: Docker & Container Best Practices
summary: Multi-stage builds, layer caching, minimal images, security scanning, and optimizing Spring Boot containers.

sections.content:
## Why Containers?

Containers package an application with all its dependencies into a single, portable artifact that runs identically in development, staging, and production. Unlike VMs, containers share the host OS kernel — they're lightweight (MBs vs GBs) and start in milliseconds.

sections.title: VM vs Container Architecture
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Virtual Machines
        HW1[Hardware]
        HW1 --> HostOS1[Host OS]
        HostOS1 --> Hypervisor[Hypervisor]
        Hypervisor --> GuestOS1[Guest OS 1\n~1GB]
        Hypervisor --> GuestOS2[Guest OS 2\n~1GB]
        GuestOS1 --> App1[App]
        GuestOS2 --> App2[App]
    end
    subgraph Containers
        HW2[Hardware]
        HW2 --> HostOS2[Host OS]
        HostOS2 --> DockerEngine[Container Runtime]
        DockerEngine --> C1[Container 1\n~50MB]
        DockerEngine --> C2[Container 2\n~50MB]
        DockerEngine --> C3[Container 3\n~50MB]
    end
    style DockerEngine fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## Optimized Spring Boot Dockerfile

```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Build
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app

# Copy pom.xml and download dependencies first (cache layer)
COPY pom.xml .
RUN mvn dependency:go-offline -q

# Copy source and build
COPY src ./src
RUN mvn package -DskipTests -q

# Stage 2: Extract Spring Boot layers
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk-alpine AS extract
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /app/target/*.jar app.jar
RUN java -Djarmode=layertools -jar app.jar extract

# Stage 3: Final minimal runtime image
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine
WORKDIR /app

# Security: run as non-root
RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup
USER appuser

# Spring Boot layer ordering: dependencies change least, app most
COPY --from=extract /app/dependencies/ ./
COPY --from=extract /app/spring-boot-loader/ ./
COPY --from=extract /app/snapshot-dependencies/ ./
COPY --from=extract /app/application/ ./

EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-XX:+UseContainerSupport", "-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75", "org.springframework.boot.loader.launch.JarLauncher"]
```

## Layer Caching

Docker builds images in layers. Each instruction creates a layer. Layers are cached — only changed layers are rebuilt.

**Key insight**: put things that change rarely at the top, frequently-changing things at the bottom:

```dockerfile
# BAD — source changes break dependency cache
COPY . .
RUN mvn dependency:go-offline
RUN mvn package

# GOOD — dependencies cached separately from source
COPY pom.xml .             # ← changes rarely → cached
RUN mvn dependency:go-offline  # ← only re-runs if pom.xml changes
COPY src ./src              # ← changes often
RUN mvn package             # ← always rebuilds (fast, deps cached)
```

**With Spring Boot layers**: the `layertools` extracts the jar into:
1. `dependencies/` — rarely changes (cache well)
2. `spring-boot-loader/` — Spring framework (rarely changes)
3. `snapshot-dependencies/` — SNAPSHOT deps
4. `application/` — your code (changes most often, only layer rebuilt)

sections.title: Spring Boot Layered Jar
sections.content:
graph TB
    JAR[Spring Boot Fat JAR] -->|layertools extract| L1[Layer 1: dependencies]
    JAR --> L2[Layer 2: spring-boot-loader]
    JAR --> L3[Layer 3: snapshot-dependencies]
    JAR --> L4[Layer 4: application]
    L1 -->|rebuild when pom changes| Cache1[Cached in Docker]
    L4 -->|rebuilds on every code change| NCache[Always rebuilt]
    style L4 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style L1 fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Container Security Best Practices

1. **Run as non-root** — never run containers as root
2. **Use minimal base images** — `eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine` instead of full JDK
3. **Scan for vulnerabilities** — `docker scout cve`, Trivy, Snyk
4. **Don't bake secrets** — use environment variables or secrets manager, not ENV in Dockerfile
5. **Read-only filesystem** — `--read-only` flag; only mount writable volumes where needed
6. **Distroless images** — Google's distroless: no shell, minimal attack surface

```bash
# Scan with Trivy
trivy image registry/order-service:1.2.0

# Run with security options
docker run \
  --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp \
  --security-opt no-new-privileges \
  --user 1001:1001 \
  order-service:1.2.0
```

sections.title: Use -XX:+UseContainerSupport
sections.content: The JVM defaults to using machine RAM for heap sizing. In containers this causes OOMKills because it doesn't see container limits. Use -XX:+UseContainerSupport (default on Java 8u191+) and -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 to make the JVM respect container memory limits.

sections.question: Why should dependency downloading be in a separate Dockerfile layer from source code copying?
options: Dependencies must be installed before source code can be compiled
So that Docker layer caching reuses the dependency layer when only source code changes — avoiding slow dependency downloads on every build
To reduce the final image size
Because Docker requires this ordering for Java applications
sections.explanation: Docker caches each layer. If you copy pom.xml and run `mvn dependency:go-offline` as separate steps before copying source, those layers are cached. When you only change source code, Docker reuses the cached dependency layer and only re-runs compilation — turning a 3-minute build into a 30-second one.
